source.title: Designing of a multi-epitopes based vaccine against Haemophilius parainfluenzae and its validation through integrated computational approaches

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to design a multi-epitope vaccine against Haemophilus parainfluenzae using computational methods. The problem is the need for a vaccine due to antibiotic resistance and the bacterium's role in various infections. The approach is to identify conserved, antigenic, non-allergenic, non-toxic T-cell and B-cell epitopes from the proteome, construct a multi-epitope vaccine with an adjuvant and linkers, and validate it through structural analysis, docking with TLR4, and immune simulation.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Bacterial infections remain a major cause of death; vaccination is key. Gram-negative bacteria like H. parainfluenzae pose challenges due to antibiotic resistance. H. parainfluenzae causes various infections. In silico approaches expedite vaccine design. The goal is to use in silico methods to identify vaccine targets from H. parainfluenzae proteome, predict T and B cell epitopes, select highly antigenic and conserved epitopes, add linkers and adjuvant, and evaluate the vaccine's properties, docking with TLR-4, and in-silico cloning.
process_signals: Problem: need for vaccine against H. parainfluenzae due to antibiotic resistance and clinical significance.
Rationale: in silico methods reduce time, labor, costs, and animal model dependence.
Hypothesis: multi-epitope approach can elicit broad immune response.

section_gists.heading: Material and method
section_gists.gist: This section is empty in the provided text.

section_gists.heading: Protein sequence retrieval and conserved region identification
section_gists.gist: Proteome downloaded from UniProt. To avoid autoimmune response, proteins lacking homology with human proteins were selected using BlastP. Antigenicity assessed with VaxiJen server (threshold 0.05).
process_signals: Constraint: avoid autoimmune response by excluding human-homologous proteins.
Filtering: antigenicity threshold 0.05.

section_gists.heading: CTL epitope prediction
section_gists.gist: CTL epitopes predicted using CTLPred for MHC class I. Selected epitopes with consensus scores below 2 (lower score indicates better MHC-I binding). Antigenicity checked with VaxiJen 2.0. Allergenicity and toxicity predicted with AllerTOP 2.0 and ToxinPred.
process_signals: Selection criterion: consensus score < 2 for better MHC-I binding.
Safety filters: non-allergenic, non-toxic.

section_gists.heading: HTL- epitope prediction
section_gists.gist: HTL epitopes are crucial for coordinating immune responses. The section mentions their role but does not detail the prediction method or selection criteria in the provided text.
process_signals: HTL epitopes work with B cells and CTL to combat pathogens.

section_gists.heading: Prediction and evaluation of B-cell epitopes
section_gists.gist: Linear B-cell epitopes predicted using ABCPred with threshold 0.5. Antigenicity, toxicity, allergenicity assessed with VaxiJen 2.0, ToxinPred, AllerTOP 2.0.
process_signals: Selection: ABCPred threshold 0.5.
Safety filters: non-toxic, non-allergenic.

section_gists.heading: MEV construction
section_gists.gist: Multi-epitope vaccine constructed by integrating adjuvant (cholera enterotoxin subunit B) at N-terminal via EAAAK linker. CTL epitopes linked with AAY, HTL with GPGPG, B-cell with KK linkers.
process_signals: Design choice: adjuvant at N-terminal for immune response modulation.
Linker choices: EAAAK for adjuvant, AAY for CTL, GPGPG for HTL, KK for B-cell epitopes.

section_gists.heading: Structure analyzing
section_gists.gist: MEV sequence checked for homology to human proteins via BlastP to avoid autoimmunity. Antigenicity and immunogenicity evaluated with VaxiJen and IEDB. Physicochemical properties (MW, pI, instability index, half-life, aliphatic index, GRAVY) computed with ProtParam. Toxicity and allergenicity predicted with ToxinPred and AllerTOP.
process_signals: Safety: no significant human homology.
Efficacy indicators: antigenicity, immunogenicity.
Stability indicators: instability index, aliphatic index, GRAVY.

section_gists.heading: Tertiary structure prediction, refinement and validation
section_gists.gist: 3D structure predicted with AlphaFold2, refined with GalaxyRefine, validated with RAMPAGE (stereochemical quality) and ProSA-web (overall quality; lower score better).
process_signals: Refinement step to improve accuracy.
Validation: RAMPAGE for stereochemistry, ProSA for overall quality.

section_gists.heading: Prediction of discontinuous B-cell epitopes
section_gists.gist: Discontinuous B-cell epitopes predicted on the 3D structure using ElliPro. Higher PI scores indicate better antibody binding potential.
process_signals: Epitope mapping on 3D structure for antibody interaction.

section_gists.heading: Disulfide engineering
section_gists.gist: Disulfide bonds introduced to enhance stability using Design v2.0 server, which predicts optimal cysteine pairs based on sequence and structure.
process_signals: Stability enhancement through disulfide engineering.
methods: Design v2.0 for disulfide bond prediction

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking with host immune receptor
section_gists.gist: Molecular docking of MEV with TLR4 performed to assess binding. TLR4 recognizes PAMPs and is crucial for immune response.
process_signals: Interaction with TLR4 indicates potential to activate innate immunity.
methods: Molecular docking (tool not specified in text)

section_gists.heading: Normal mode analysis of MEV-receptor docked complex
section_gists.gist: Stability of MEV-TLR4 complex assessed via normal mode analysis using iMODS. Parameters: eigenvalues (lower = more deformable), covariance, B-factors (higher = more flexible), deformability.
process_signals: Stability assessment of docked complex.
Eigenvalues indicate energy required for deformation.

section_gists.heading: Immune simulation
section_gists.gist: Immune response to MEV simulated using C-ImmSim to predict efficacy without in vivo/in vitro experiments.
process_signals: In silico validation of immune response.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research follows a linear computational pipeline: proteome retrieval → epitope prediction (CTL, HTL, B-cell) → filtering for antigenicity, non-allergenicity, non-toxicity → vaccine construction with adjuvant and linkers → structural analysis and validation → docking with TLR4 → stability analysis → immune simulation.
The hypothesis is that a multi-epitope vaccine with carefully selected epitopes and an adjuvant can elicit a protective immune response against H. parainfluenzae.
No iterative design changes or failures are reported; the process appears to be a single-pass selection and validation.
uncertainties_to_resolve: The HTL epitope prediction method and selection criteria are not described in the provided text.
The molecular docking tool and parameters are not specified.
No quantitative results (e.g., docking scores, immune simulation outcomes) are included in these sections.
The paper lacks discussion of any discarded epitopes or refinement steps beyond the standard pipeline.
Wet-lab validation is not performed, so the computational predictions remain unverified.

events.summary: The study hypothesizes that a multi-epitope vaccine can be designed using bioinformatics and immuno-informatic methodologies.
Vaccine design involves selecting non-toxic, non-allergenic B and T cell epitopes with high population coverage.
Multi-epitope constructs were designed using suitable linker sequences and an adjuvant.
3D structure of the vaccine was modeled, refined, and validated in silico.
Molecular docking and dynamics simulation were performed to assess binding with TLR4 and complex stability.
Experimental validation is still required to confirm immunogenicity and efficacy.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the abstract and introduction, outlining the problem, hypothesis, design, and computational steps, and ends with a limitation about needing experimental validation.

events.summary: Tertiary structure of the vaccine was predicted using AlphaFold2 and refined with GalaxyRefine.
Refined structure validated with RAMPAGE and ProSA-web; Ramachandran plot showed 90.3% residues in favored regions.
Disulfide engineering performed using Design v2.0 to improve protein stability.
Molecular docking of MEV with TLR4 performed to assess binding and potential immune activation.
Normal mode analysis of MEV-TLR4 complex using iMODS to assess stability; parameters include eigenvalues, covariance, B-factors, deformability.
Immune simulation performed with C-ImmSim to predict immune responses to the MEV construct.
Population coverage of selected T-cell epitopes estimated; worldwide coverage 90.00%, highest in Japan at 98.41%.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers tertiary structure prediction, refinement, validation, B-cell epitope prediction, disulfide engineering, molecular docking, normal mode analysis, immune simulation, and population coverage. All events are process-oriented and supported by quotes.

events.summary: Secondary structure prediction of the final multi-epitope vaccine construct using SOPMA tool.
Ramachandran plot assessment revealed 90.3% of residues in favored regions, indicating good stereochemical quality.
Disulfide engineering identified three residue pairs for cysteine introduction to enhance stability.
Immune simulation showed effective primary and secondary responses with increased IgM, IgG1, IgG2 antibodies.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers structural validation, disulfide engineering, docking, normal mode analysis, immune simulation, and cloning. All events are process-oriented and supported by quotes.

events.summary: Normal mode analysis of MEV-TLR4 docked complex performed using iMODS, reporting deformability, B-factor, covariance, elastic network, and eigenvalues.
In silico immune simulation using C-ImmSim shows cytokine and immunoglobulin responses to MEV antigen over 35 days.
Codon optimization for E. coli K12 host performed; CAI=0.9 and GC content=49.32% achieved.
Selected epitopes cover around 90% of the world's population combined.
In-silico designed vaccine constructs for other pathogens have undergone experimental validation, confirming potential efficacy.
Predicted physicochemical characteristics indicate increased hydrophilicity, stability, good solubility, low molecular weight, and thermodynamic stability.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains results from normal mode analysis, immune simulation, codon optimization, and discussion of expected vaccine properties and population coverage.

events.summary: The study acknowledges that in-silico designed vaccine constructs have undergone experimental validation in other contexts, confirming potential efficacy.
It is expected that the vaccination models will exhibit strong antigenicity with minimal allergenicity and toxicity.
Predicted physicochemical characteristics indicate increased hydrophilicity and stability of the vaccine designs.
The models show good solubility, low molecular weight, and thermodynamic stability, suggesting effective production and administration.
Ramachandran plot values provided evidence of substantial improvement and desired characteristics in the quality and stability of the vaccine constructs.
Molecular docking analyses were performed to evaluate interactions of H. parainfluenzae vaccines with human TLR4 receptors.
Repeated exposure to the antigenic vaccine construct resulted in activation of stronger immune responses, including stimulation of Helper T cells and development of memory B and T cells.
The construction of immune-informatics-based vaccines heavily relies on prediction methods, introducing uncertainties regarding the level of protection.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains discussion and validation of the vaccine design, including expected properties, structural validation, docking, immune response simulation, and limitations. It provides process events such as hypothesis, analysis, experiment, and limitation.
This chunk contains only reference citations and no research process events.

evidence_bank.quote: The principal objective of this investigation is to utilize bioinformatics and immuno-informatic methodologies in the development of a candidate multi-epitope Vaccine.
the chosen alleles aim to provide coverage for 90% of the global population
Multi-epitope constructs were designed by using suitable linker sequences. To enhance the immunological potential, an adjuvant sequence was incorporated using the EAAAK linker.
The final vaccine construct, comprising 344 amino acids, was achieved after the addition of adjuvants and linkers.
The three-dimensional conformation underwent modeling and refinement, validated through in-silico methods.
a protein-protein molecular docking analysis was conducted to predict effective binding poses between the multi-epitope Vaccine and the Toll-like receptor 4 protein.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Design a multi-epitope vaccine against Haemophilus parainfluenzae using computational methods to address antibiotic resistance and lack of existing vaccine.

canonical_events.summary: Hypothesis that a multi-epitope vaccine designed via bioinformatics can elicit broad immune response.
Proteome retrieval and selection of non-human-homologous, antigenic proteins (VaxiJen threshold 0.05).
CTL epitope prediction using CTLPred (consensus score <2), filtered for antigenicity, non-allergenicity, non-toxicity.
B-cell epitope prediction using ABCPred (threshold 0.5), filtered for antigenicity, non-allergenicity, non-toxicity.

Multi-epitope vaccine construction: adjuvant (cholera enterotoxin subunit B) at N-terminal via EAAAK linker; CTL epitopes linked with AAY, HTL with GPGPG, B-cell with KK; final construct 344 amino acids.
Physicochemical and safety analysis of MEV: no human homology, antigenic, immunogenic, non-toxic, non-allergenic; ProtParam computed MW, pI, instability index, half-life, aliphatic index, GRAVY.
3D structure prediction with AlphaFold2, refinement with GalaxyRefine.
Structure validation: Ramachandran plot showed 90.3% residues in favored regions, 8.3% allowed, 1.3% outliers; ProSA-web Z-score acceptable.
Disulfide engineering using Design v2.0/DbD2: three residue pairs (PRO74-HIS78, GLY223-GLN245, PHE246-ALA264) selected for cysteine introduction to enhance stability.
Molecular docking of MEV with TLR4 to assess binding and potential immune activation.
Normal mode analysis of MEV-TLR4 complex using iMODS: eigenvalues (e.g., 2.277729e-05), covariance, B-factors, deformability indicate stability.
Immune simulation with C-ImmSim: effective primary and secondary responses; IgM+IgG peak at 650,000 at day 15; IFN-g peak at 400,000 at day 5; memory B and T cell development.
Population coverage analysis: selected epitopes cover 90% worldwide, highest in Japan (98.41%).
Codon optimization for E. coli K12: CAI=0.9, GC content=49.32%, clone size 5788 bp in pET30a(+).
Experimental validation required to confirm immunogenicity and efficacy; reliance on prediction methods introduces uncertainties.
